sections.name: 2.1 Email Positioning
sections.content:
| Element | Specification |
|---------|---------------|
| Flow | PP-Education D0-21 (Flow 11) |
| Email # | E4 (Day 21) |
| Type | Cross-Level (R1-R6) |
| Phase | Wave 5 — Post-Purchase Education |
| Timing | Day 21 post-purchase (three-week milestone) |
| Primary Goal | Check-in + reinforce habit formation + set up for extended upsell |
| Secondary Goal | Prevent disengagement at the 3-week plateau |
| Emotional Arc | Open recognition → Validation of position → Low-bar win celebration → Permission-based next step |

sections.name: 2.2 R1-R6 Calibration Check
sections.content:
| Rule | Application in This Email |
|------|--------------------------|
| R1 — Symptom Over Label | Never uses "Level 1/2/3" or "ASD." Uses symptom descriptions: "verbal and can say wet," "non-verbal and show you with a gesture," "pulled at their pants." |
| R2 — 3-5 Recognition Anchors | Six anchors in the recognition block covering the full spectrum: pause-after (L1/L2), nothing (L3/deep hypo), step-toward (L2-L3), pants-pull (L2), pre-event register (L1-L2), same-as-week-1 (any). Every reader finds 1-2 that match. |
| R3 — Age Ranges | Opens with "Whether your child is 3 or 10." Never pins to a single age. Testimonials span ages 4, 6, 8. |
| R4 — Verbal/Non-Verbal Inclusivity | "Verbal and can say 'wet' OR non-verbal and show you with a gesture." Every option paired. Three-Family testimonial covers verbal (Sarah), mixed (Lisa), non-verbal (Maria). |
| R5 — Timeline Flexibility | "Some kids process the sensation in 2 weeks. Some need 8. Both normal." Never promises a fixed outcome. Week 4-6 as "when signals strengthen" — never "will happen by." |
| R6 — Low-Bar Success Metrics | 11 distinct mini-wins listed. Includes a pause, a look, a hand touch, a step, 2 hours dry, a signal or word, less resistance. Success is defined as any observable awareness — not "fully trained." |

sections.name: 2.3 "Whether...Or...If..." Pattern Application
sections.content:
The opening paragraph uses the three-part inclusive bridge:

> "Whether your child is 3 or 10. Whether they're verbal and can say 'wet' or non-verbal and show you with a gesture. Whether you've seen one pause or zero. If your child wears underwear that gives their nervous system feedback it's been missing, here's where you might be right now."

This structure simultaneously:
1. Acknowledges age diversity (R3)
2. Covers verbal and non-verbal modes (R4)
3. Covers full outcome spectrum from first signs to nothing (R5, R6)
4. Uses "Whether" + "Whether" + "If..." pattern consistently

sections.name: 2.4 Three-Family Testimonial Pattern (GF Inclusion)
sections.content:
The testimonial block features three named families, Sarah, Lisa, Maria, each with a distinct child profile:

| Family | Child Profile | Email Position | Function |
|--------|---------------|----------------|----------|
| Sarah | Verbal 4yo, "almost trained" | L1 anchor | L1 mom finds her story, sees Week 3 validated |
| Lisa | Mixed 6yo, BCBA context | L2 anchor | L2 mom sees mechanism-specific language, BCBA co-sign |
| Maria | Non-verbal 8yo, high support | L3 anchor | L3 mom gets dignity-first framing, "enough for now" |

The pattern ends with: *"Three different kids. Three different timelines. One common experience: the body-signal connection is building."*, This creates a unifying frame that all 3 levels and GF sub-groups share.

sections.name: 2.5 GF Sub-Group Compatibility
sections.content:
| Sub-Group | How This Email Serves Them |
|-----------|---------------------------|
| A — Diagnostic Entrants | No diagnosis assumed. Symptom-only language. "Whether...Or...If..." frame does not require level knowledge. |
| B — Diagnosis-Rejecters | No level labels anywhere. Uses "nervous system" framing. Never "autism" or "ASD." "Your child" not "your autistic child." |
| C — Sensory-Difference (SPD/ADHD) | Mechanism language works cross-diagnosis. "Nervous system feedback" is label-agnostic. Science framing compatible with OT concepts. |
| D — Overwhelmed Non-Selectors | Recognition anchors across all levels let her find herself. Three-Family pattern gives her a story to self-identify with. Migration anchors built in naturally. |
| E — Cart/Browse No Opt-In | Low-context friendly. Mechanism reminder is self-contained. Timeline flexibility reduces anxiety. Guarantee present via Lena P.S. |

sections.name: 2.6 Sign-Off Rationale
sections.content:
Closing: "With understanding,", This the standard sign-off for emails 3-5 in a flow (per brand voice rules). PP-Edu E4 is the 4th email in the PP-Education flow.

P.S. Variant: Long, includes Lena's personal story (son trained at 8), normalizes Week 3 plateau, reinforces 60-day guarantee with permission frame. This appropriate for late-flow emails where trust depth > brevity.

sections.name: 2.7 Extended Upsell Setup
sections.content:
The email concludes with an "exit options" triage that includes a soft bridge to the next stage:

> "If you want to continue with more structured support as your child moves into weeks 4-8, we have resources designed for the next stage of body-signal learning."

This prepares for:
- PP-Extended Upsell (Flow 12), 1-Pair → 3+3, 3+3 → 5+5 upgrades starting Day 30
- PP-Mid Check-In (Flow 13), Day 30, Day 45, Day 60 educational touchpoints
- PP-Extended Education (Flow 22), Level-specific deeper education (tags permitting)

The framing is educational, not promotional: "resources designed for the next stage" rather than "buy more."

sections.name: 2.8 Forbidden Language Check
sections.content:
| Forbidden Item | Status |
|----------------|--------|
| Level labels (Level 1/2/3, ASD, autism) | ✅ Absent |
| "Easy," "simple," "quick" | ✅ Absent |
| Fixed ages | ✅ Age ranges throughout |
| Outcome promises ("will train," "guaranteed") | ✅ Absent |
| NT comparison ("like other kids") | ✅ Absent |
| Cheerleader language ("don't give up!") | ✅ Absent |
| Grief as sales tool | ✅ Absent |
| "Fix," "cure," "solve" | ✅ Absent |
| Pre-K urgency (L2-L3 forbidden) | ✅ Absent |
| Exclamation mark proliferation | ✅ Minimal, natural use |
| "Miracle," "breakthrough," "amazing" | ✅ Absent |

sections.name: 2.9 Tone & Energy Calibration
sections.content:
| Dimension | Setting |
|-----------|---------|
| Hope/Realism | 50/50 (cross-level standard) |
| Energy | Measured, reflective — a check-in, not a push |
| Peer Voice | Lena as fellow mom who's been at this stage |
| Science Density | Low-Medium — mechanism reminder, not deep dive |
| Urgency | Zero — permission-based exit options |
| CTA Force | Minimal — informational, not promotional |
| Pacing | Spacious — the 3-week mark deserves reflection space |

sections.name: 2.10 Subject Line Rationale
sections.content:
Primary: "3 weeks in, what are you seeing?"

- Question hook type (Type 5 per the 6 subject line formulas)
- Creates curiosity to open and self-assess
- Cross-level appropriate, "what are you seeing" works whether the answer is "a lot" or "nothing"
- No urgency, no promise, no label
- Fits the check-in emotional arc

sections.name: 2.11 Section Map
sections.content:
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ PP-EDU E4, SECTION MAP │
│ Day 21, Three-Week Check-In │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ │
│ Subject Line: Question hook, "3 weeks in, what are you seeing?" │
│ Preheader: Inclusive permission frame │
│ │
│ ① OBSERVATION (R3) │
│ Whether...Or...If... pattern, age, verbal/non-verbal, outcome │
│ ↓ │
│ ② RECOGNITION ANCHORS (R2, R4, R6) │
│ 6 specific symptom situations spanning all levels │
│ ↓ │
│ ③ VALIDATION (R5) │
│ "Nothing in week 3 is not failure, neurology on its timeline" │
│ ↓ │
│ ④ THREE-FAMILY TESTIMONIAL PATTERN │
│ Sarah (L1) → Lisa (L2) → Maria (L3) → unified conclusion │
│ ↓ │
│ ⑤ MECHANISM REMINDER │
│ Body-Signal Learning Layer repetition → neural pathway building │
│ ↓ │
│ ⑥ LOW-BAR WINS (R6) │
│ 11 mini-wins to document, neural pathways becoming visible │
│ ↓ │
│ ⑦ EXIT OPTIONS / EXTENDED UPSELL SETUP │
│ Three triage paths: signs → next stage / nothing → within range │
│ / unsure → deeper guidance available │
│ ↓ │
│ ⑧ LENA SIGN-OFF (With understanding,) + P.S. (60-day guarantee) │
│ │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

sections.name: 2.12 Success Metrics
sections.content:
| Metric | Target | Notes |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Open rate | >50% | Strong subject line for engaged recipients |
| Click rate (any) | 8-12% | Recognition anchors drive identification clicks |
| Click on extended upsell link | 3-5% | Educational framing, not hard upsell |
| Reply rate | 1-3% | "What are you seeing?" invites response |
| Unsubscribe rate | <0.5% | Low-bar framing + permission reduces opt-outs |
| Migration clicks (GF → level story) | 2-4% | Embedded in Three-Family testimonials |

e2.raw: meta: { part: "Part 2 of 6", timing: "+24 hours", theme: "The Mechanism"

e3.raw: meta: { part: "Part 3 of 6", timing: "+2 days", theme: "Absolution"

e4.raw: meta: { part: "Part 4 of 6", timing: "+4 days", theme: "The Product"

e5.raw: meta: { part: "Part 5 of 6", timing: "+6 days", theme: "One Story"

why.raw:
label: "Why we chose this story",
      body: "Sarah isn't our best case. She's our most common one. Her situation is close to what many families find — a child on the spectrum, a couple of years of tried methods, a low point that felt like an ending, and then a method that finally matched the map.",

e6.raw: meta: { part: "Part 6 of 6", timing: "+8 days", theme: "The Invitation"

covered.raw:
label: "What's covered",
      items: [
        "Your child refusing to wear them — full refund.",
        "No visible progress in 60 days — full refund.",
        "A regression during the trial period — full refund.",
        "Any reason at all you decide it's not the right fit — full refund.",
      ],

signoff.raw:
line1: "With you,",
    name: "Lena Bauer",
    ps: "P.S. If you ever want to reply and tell me about your child — I read every one. Really.",

footer.raw:
tagline: "Calm progress, one day at a time.",
    links: ["Our Method", "Shop the Pants", "Parent Stories"],
    address: "BrightKidCo Ltd · Berlin, Germany · hello@brightkidco.com",
